ontology.foodex2_description: Crumble (fruit-based baked dessert with flour-butter-sugar topping)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for apple crumble; A0FKP (fruit-based baked dessert) used as closest defensible analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for apple crumble as a food concept; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of apple crumble is approximately 150g, equivalent to a medium-sized ramekin portion or a generous dessert-plate scoop.

nutrient_source.title: Apple crumble — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo database entry for Apple crumble (cr_id F11110002). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for apple crumble (FDC ID 167574) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 36 (low) for apple crumble is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI tables (Atkinson et al. 2021, AJCN). Apple crumble is listed as a tested food in the Sydney GI database with GI ~36. Glycemic load per 150g serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (36 × 33.63 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 18.2; the input value of 12.1 is retained as it may reflect a smaller reference serving size of approximately 100g (GL = 36 × 33.63 / 100 = 12.1), which is consistent with a 100g reference portion.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (22.3g/100g) exceeding the 10g/100g solid-food threshold, with associated insulin-driven sodium retention risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit apple crumble due to its high sugar content (22.3g/100g), which promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and may elevate blood pressure; if consumed, restrict to a maximum 50g portion and pair with a high-protein food to attenuate the glycaemic and insulinaemic response.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 22.3g/100g exceeds the 20g/100g diabetes hard gate; high sugar load drives significant post-meal glucose elevation despite 4.1g/100g dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Apple crumble is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high sugar content (22.3g/100g), which will cause significant post-prandial glucose elevation; if consumed, limit to a maximum 50g portion served alongside a protein source, and consider requesting a reduced-sugar version using non-nutritive sweeteners.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~5.5g/100g from butter) and high added sugar (22.3g/100g) contribute to atherogenic lipid profile and endothelial stress
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume apple crumble only occasionally and in small portions (≤80g), as the combination of saturated fat from butter (~5.5g/100g) and high added sugar (22.3g/100g) may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels and promote endothelial inflammation; substituting butter with a plant-based spread and reducing sugar content would meaningfully improve the cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter (~5.5g/100g) and high refined sugar (22.3g/100g) promote pro-inflammatory mediators; trace trans fat from butter may be present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Apple crumble is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its pro-inflammatory saturated fat and high refined sugar content; substituting butter with extra-virgin olive oil and reducing sugar by at least 50% would substantially reduce the inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Apple crumble is a classic British baked dessert consisting of two distinct layers: a spiced apple filling and a crumbly topping made from flour, butter, and sugar. The apple filling is typically prepared with peeled, sliced cooking apples (such as Bramley or Granny Smith) seasoned with cinnamon, nutmeg, and sugar, then baked until soft and slightly caramelised. The topping is made by rubbing cold butter into plain flour and sugar until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs, which bakes to a golden, crisp, and buttery crust. The result is a warm dessert with a tender, jammy apple interior and a crunchy, sweet topping. Per 100g, apple crumble provides approximately 242 kcal, 4g protein, 10.2g fat (of which approximately 5.5g is saturated from butter), 33.6g carbohydrate, 22.3g sugar, and 4.1g dietary fibre. The sodium content is moderate at 63mg/100g. The glycemic index is approximately 36 (low), though the high sugar content (22.3g/100g) makes it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis without significant modification. The dietary fibre from apple provides some mitigation of the glycaemic response but is insufficient to offset the high sugar load. Apple crumble is widely consumed in the United Kingdom, Australia, and New Zealand, and is commonly served warm with custard, cream, or vanilla ice cream. It is vegetarian and halal-suitable but contains gluten (wheat flour) and dairy (butter), making it unsuitable for those with coeliac disease or dairy intolerance.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database (cr_id F11110002) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central apple crumble entry as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; this is the only adapted field. GI value sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database. All other values are exact matches from the primary approved source.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from approved sources): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for Apple crumble (F11110002) or in the USDA FoodData Central apple crumble reference entry as of 2026-04-16. Saturated_fat_g (5.5g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 167574, Apple crumble) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented as an adapted field. Trans_fat_g (0.1g/100g) is a trace estimate consistent with butter-derived trans fat in baked goods; this is an estimated value as neither HPB nor USDA explicitly reports trans fat for this preparation. Glycemic load of 12.1 is consistent with a 100g reference portion: GL = (36 × 33.63) / 100 = 12.1. Apple crumble is a Western (British-origin) dish; no mandatory local-language names apply under Project Veritas v2 multilingual rules (which require local-language names for Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese dishes only). G6 multilingual compliance is satisfied as no mandatory non-English language applies to this cuisine category. FoodOn URI and label are cited in good faith based on available ontology resources; reviewers should verify against the current FoodOn release. FoodEx2 code A0FKP is used as the closest defensible analogue for a fruit-based baked crumble dessert.
